Having been asked about it many times, somewhere in your notebook you're going to find, huh, I don't know how I got myself out of order but I did. But somewhere in your notebook there is a pink page, or there should be a pink page, that I don't know where it is. Isn't this awful? One-O-Five. Is it one-O-Five? Good for you. One-O-Six. One-O-Five. I'm sure glad I numbered the pages. It would have been a real mess if I had numbered the pages, huh? Yeah, thank you. One-O-Five and one-O-Six. I've been asked about it many times and so if enough want to do it, late in the year I'll do a workshop just on that. What I would call influential writing. And we will dissect and take apart things and show you why they're done the way they are. Get you some formulas you can work off of. Workshop your stuff. Spend time just on this. If enough of you want to do it, I will be happy to do it and to do the preparatory work to do it. You'll come out of it better organized, more conscious of why I do what I do the way I do it and how you can emulate it in your own style. You'll come out of it with some templates and some formulas. You'll come out of it with some checklist that will be a benefit to you on an ongoing basis. And you'll come out with your stuff heavily and thoroughly critiqued, painfully in front of other people. And improved so that you have a better model of your own stuff to work from. Honest to God doesn't matter to me whether we do it or not. So it really is entirely up to you. Not up to me. You won't see me beat it up. You're up. I just have a complex question. Doesn't matter if it's complex as long as it's quick. You've mentioned a few different times not to do written proposals, but then you also to say to have everything in writing. Where does that line come in where you put it down on paper? Well, first of all, when I say I mean everything in writing, I mean all of the control elements. So we're never going to work without a contract, for example. And incredibly a lot of people do. We're never going to, we try never to do anything without a paper trail. And by that I mean if you and I have a conversation while you're here, or we have a conversation on the phone, that in any way affects the work that I'm doing for you. You're going to get a follow up fact for me that says while we were in Reston, you mentioned X to me and I said we would do Y. And I just wanted to verify that that's what we're doing. Now, that doesn't require a response. I'm not asking you to sign off on anything, but I have paper trailed our conversation because we will never have, you won't have a memory of what our conversation was a week a month a year from now. And I at least want to be able to pull out a piece of paper. My philosophy on proposals is that the very premise of them is wrong. So some of this is semantic, but some of it is beyond semantic. Your doctor doesn't give you a proposal, he gives you a prescription. And so he doesn't propose that you lose 25 pounds and get on this prescription drug. He says here, go get this drug, right? So in the same way, behaviorally, philosophically and semantically, we don't want to propose things to them. We want to prescribe things to them. And the proposal document really is a bad sales presentation. You know, it's really what it is. So at bare minimum, what you want to be doing is semantic changes to it so that you're delivering the action plan or the work plan or almost anything but a proposal. But more importantly is to try and get to a point where you go from consultation to contract with no stopping in between. So for example, in everybody I work with, at the end of that day, we have outlined, we have identified what they're going to go do, sort of their game plan. And if there's a part of it that I am going to deliver now has work product, in my case it's copyrighting, we've agreed on what that project's going to be.
